Just a quick comment on the question James asked earlier about the significance between using a MinMax scaler or a standard scaler.
The points I've seen are kind of technical, and I don't see a very intuitive reason behind it, but usually we use MinMax scaler when we know the bound of our data.
So like, we know that it's a fixed region where the values would be coming from.
Suppose if you have an image, you know that the pixel value can vary between 0 and 255.
In places like these, we usually use MinMax scaler because it is sensitive to outliers, we know that the values would be coming from a fixed region, and there would be no outliers.
That's why we make use of MinMax scaler; it reduces the values between zero and one.
Standard scaler is used when we know that the feature that we are using is normally distributed, so the data distribution is normal.
What the standard scaler does is it centers the data around zero, and it varies between some positive and some negative value.
So if you know that the feature is normally distributed, you make use of that generally.
A normal advice would be if you are working
With a dataset like images or something, think use min-max scaler if you are working with problems like the ones being looked into or any other linear regression or logistic regression.
Make use of standard scalar, but you can maybe use them alternatively as well there won't be a significant impact on the outcome of the algorithm.
As Felix asked about feature importance, it's a very interesting area of research to look into the importance of various features that are contributing to the final decision.
For various algorithms, different strategies are used.
For instance, if we talk about linear regression, we can have an idea about what features are important by looking at the coefficients of the features.

The coefficient which is larger we can simply say that it is being paid more attention to, and if the coefficient is smaller we see that it's not paying significant contribution to the final decision.
But that thing is not very solid because it depends upon the scale of the feature.
If the feature range is very high, it is dependent upon the range or the scale of the feature.
So that's why there are other methods that are used in order to make sense of what features are important.
Decision trees and gradient boosting make use of something that provides a feature which I can quickly show you how to do.
For other algorithms like neural networks, since we don't have lots of visibility into how it's making its decisions, that's why there have been recent attempts to interpret the model.
There's a package or a technique called SHAP or Shapley values which I usually use.
There's another technique called LIME.
The technical details of it are derived from what is called Game Theory approach.

We can talk about it sometimes, but if we get some time, you can make use of SHAP or LIME in order to see how a complicated algorithm is making its decision for simpler algorithms like linear regression or logistic regression.
You can make use of the coefficients just to have an idea, and for XGBoost they have their internal
this method built into their package, which can show you what features are important.
But before that, a quick comment about this: could you just go back one minute and explain why, if the input data is normalized, why would the coefficients be affected by the range?
Wouldn't the coefficients be sufficient to know what's more important?
Yeah, so even if it is normalized, you can do that, so it can give you a hint about what feature is important.
But still, I think it is usually not considered a very good approach because it is just simply taking the normal correlation of the feature.
If it is increasing, it is just depending upon the normal correlation between the values.
Sometimes normal correlation, which is something that Felix asked about earlier, like the correlation analysis that Ben presented, it is just kind of giving you a limited picture of what's happening.

The features could combine with each other in certain ways where normal correlations wouldn't give you the full picture, but if you combine them in intelligent ways, it can come up with something that is more useful.
So maybe, even if you normalize it, it would be a simplistic approach toward deducing if a feature is important or not.

But this is something I haven't looked into in a lot of details, so I wouldn't be sure to comment much about it, but usually it's not considered a good strategy to look into the features and decide if it is important just based on the weights of the values.
You can do that for, let's say, linear regression or logistic regression, but how would you do that for complicated algorithms where visibility is then lost?
So that could be another reason why it's not very successfully used.

So a normal practice would be to split the data before doing the
Standardization: the logic for this is if you train your algorithm on the data set that you trained it on is with you.
So when you do the normalization, you know the mean and the standard deviation of the data because you have it.
But if you are testing this on a data set, the goal is to make use of this model on unseen data.
For unseen data you don't have that mean and standard deviation.
The hope is that the distribution of the data stays the same even if you don't have it.
The overall trend stays the same, but just to be sure you don't do the standardization before the train-test split.
Just to make sure that the test data that I'm going to test this algorithm on is not having the knowledge of its mean and standard deviation.
So what we do is we first split it, we then do the standard scaler on the training data set and we make use of the same scaler to normalize the test set.
Now the information this scaler is having about the mean and standard deviation is not about the test set.
It is that information comes from the training set which it has seen and we make use of that to normalize the test data.
Does this make sense?

If you quickly look at the formula for precision, you'll see it is making use of these concepts called true positives and false positives.
What is a true positive?
If an algorithm predicts that it's a positive case—positive case is usually for this example a positive class would be the malignant class—so it's usually considered the positive class.
If you are testing for something and the presence of that thing is usually considered as a positive class, you can do it the other way around as well, but usually that's how it's done.

So if we want to evaluate the precision score, true positives is when the algorithm predicts that it's a positive class case and the ground truth is also a positive class—in other words, a correct classification.
That's a true positive.
What's a false positive?
If the algorithm says it's a positive class—like it's a malignant class—but in ground truth it's not a positive class, it is a benign case, then that turns out to be a false positive.
Does this make sense?
Yeah, Felix?
Okay, so let's see what would be the precision score of an algorithm that I was talking about earlier that's just spitting out benign for every case.
How much would be true positives?
There would be zero because all the time it's saying it's a negative class—it's a benign class all the time for 100% of the examples, it's saying it's a benign class.
So precision turns out to be zero.
It correctly identifies that flaw in the model—that it is all the time just simply spitting out it's a benign case.

Talking about it without explaining it on the whiteboard—maybe I'll do that in a future session sometime, but it's about picking out those limitations in our models where accuracy wouldn't be able to see it directly.
I found it a bit confusing as well when I was looking at the equations, so I wrote down these common explanations that made sense to me.
Accuracy is overall how often the classifier is correct in its prediction.
Precision is when the classifier predicts a positive value—how often was that prediction correct, only when it predicts a positive value.
Recall is when the prediction was supposed to be positive—so when the actual value is positive, how often did the classifier get that?
Does that way of explaining it help at all?
For precision, it is testing the ability of the model to recognize a positive class—how much this algorithm is able to identify a positive class.
Accuracy wouldn't be able to do that; accuracy is just looking into whether the decision is correct or not correct.
It's not testing a specific class for you; it's not testing the accuracy of the model for the positive class.
It is overall general accuracy of how much the model is making correct predictions.
Now, if an algorithm is continuously predicting a negative class, the accuracy is 98%, but the precision is zero because it's not even saying for a single case that it's a positive class.
For all the examples, it's saying it's a negative class, so it is missing on the ability of the model to identify the correct classes.
Right, I'll talk about this in detail.
Let me just quickly show you what I was actually doing this for.
I was trying to show you the different methods that you can use to identify the importance of various features.
For some reason, it is not showing the names of the features here.
For XGBoost, it's internal plot importance—an internal mechanism of identifying which feature is more important.
That's how you
It can do it. It has this method by the name of plot importance. You can pass in your model and then there are multiple ways it can try to identify which features are important.
SHAP is one of the techniques. What is SHAP again?

It is something that XGBoost uses—the way it makes its decisions. I know it won't make any sense to you right now because we haven't talked about it, but just for queuing sake, that's how you can see which feature is important.

If the feature with the highest F score, it just simply means this feature is more important, and this is not doing something simple like just simply telling you the coefficient of the features like the one I was talking about earlier.
Coefficient analysis would just simply tell normal linear correspondence between features.
If the algorithm becomes complex like in XGBoost or neural network or any other complicated algorithm, you cannot make use of simple coefficient analysis in that place.

That's why for XGBoost, they have their own way of evaluating the importance of the features. But for other algorithms, there's a generic package which you can make use of in neural networks, for XGBoost as well, for any kind of model that they do this for.
The internal working again is derived from an approach called in Game Theory.
